title: How to Apply to University in South Africa: A Step-by-Step Guide
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Applying to university in South Africa involves multiple steps across different systems. Some universities require you to apply directly through their own portals. Others use the Central Applications Office. All of them have deadlines, document requirements, and specific admission criteria that you must meet. Missing a deadline or submitting an incomplete application can cost you a year. Here is the complete process for applying to South African universities as a matriculant.

Step One: Research Your Options Early

Start researching universities and courses in Grade 11, not in your matric year. The application deadlines for most South African universities fall between March and September of your matric year, and the most competitive courses fill quickly. A student who begins researching in Grade 11 has time to compare programs, visit campuses if possible, and prepare a strong application. A student who starts in Grade 12 is already behind.

Identify the courses that interest you and the universities that offer them. Check the admission requirements for each course: the minimum APS score, the required subjects, and the required achievement levels in those subjects. Check whether the course requires National Benchmark Tests. Check the application deadlines. Write all of this information down. The research phase is not exciting, but it prevents costly mistakes later. A student who applies for a course without meeting the subject requirements will be rejected regardless of their APS.

Consider factors beyond the course content. What is the cost of tuition and accommodation? What bursaries and scholarships are available? What is the campus culture like? What are the employment outcomes for graduates of the program? A course that looks good on paper may not be the right fit for your goals, your budget, or your preferred learning environment. Talk to current students if you can. Read student reviews. Visit the campus if it is accessible to you. The more you know before you apply, the better your decision will be.

Step Two: Register for the National Benchmark Tests

Many South African universities require National Benchmark Test results as part of the admission process. Check whether your chosen universities and courses require them. If they do, register for the NBTs early. Registration opens in April for tests that take place between May and January. The test dates fill quickly, especially in major cities. Register as soon as you know you need the tests. The NBT website has the current schedule and registration details.

Prepare for the NBTs by familiarizing yourself with the test format and practicing with sample questions. The tests assess academic literacy, quantitative literacy, and, for some applicants, mathematics. You cannot cram for them, but you can prepare by reading regularly, practicing data interpretation, and reviewing foundational mathematics concepts. The NBT results are sent directly to the universities you specify during registration. You do not need to submit them separately.

Step Three: Gather Your Documents

University applications require supporting documents. Gather these early so you are not scrambling at the deadline. You will need a certified copy of your identity document or birth certificate, your Grade 11 final results and your Grade 12 mid-year results if they are available, proof of payment of the application fee, and any additional documents required by specific universities or courses, such as a portfolio for creative programs or a motivation letter. Some universities also require a copy of your parent or guardian's identity document and proof of income if you are applying for financial aid.

Certify your documents at a police station, a post office, or a commissioner of oaths. A certified copy is a photocopy that has been stamped and signed to confirm it is a true copy of the original. Do not send original documents unless specifically instructed. They may not be returned.

Step Four: Submit Your Applications

Apply through the correct channel for each university. The University of the Witwatersrand, the University of Cape Town, Stellenbosch University, and several others accept direct applications through their online portals. The Central Applications Office handles applications for the University of KwaZulu-Natal, the University of the Western Cape, and several other institutions. Some universities accept applications through both channels. Check the specific application method for each university on your list.

Complete each application fully and accurately. An incomplete application will not be processed. Pay the application fee for each university. The fees are non-refundable and typically range from one hundred to two hundred and fifty rand. Keep proof of payment. Upload or submit your supporting documents. Submit your application before the deadline. A late application may not be considered, especially for competitive courses.

After submitting, monitor your application status through the university's online portal. Universities may request additional information or documents. Respond promptly. A delay in providing requested information can result in your application being withdrawn.

Step Five: Wait for a Response and Plan for All Outcomes

Universities typically respond to applications between October and January. You may receive a firm offer, a conditional offer that depends on your final matric results, or a rejection. If you receive a conditional offer, the conditions will specify the APS and subject levels you need to achieve in your final NSC examinations to secure your place. If you receive multiple offers, you will need to choose which one to accept. Most universities require you to accept or decline an offer by a specified date.

If you do not receive an offer from your preferred university, you have options. You can apply to other universities that have later deadlines or that have space available after the initial offer round. You can inquire about extended degree programs or bridging courses that provide an alternative pathway to your intended course. You can take a gap year to improve your matric results or to gain work experience and apply again the following year. A rejection is not the end of your education. It is a signal that you need to adjust your strategy.

The Bottom Line

Applying to South African universities requires early research, timely NBT registration, careful document preparation, and submission through the correct channels. Start researching in Grade 11. Register for the NBTs as soon as you know you need them. Gather your documents early. Submit complete applications before the deadlines. Monitor your application status and respond promptly to requests. If you do not receive your preferred offer, explore alternative courses, extended degree programs, or reapplication strategies. The application process is demanding, but it is manageable with planning and organization.
